name: Deliver Difficult News (a No, a Cancellation, a Regret)
description: Tell someone something they will not want to hear — a cancellation, a refusal, a withdrawal, a decision against them — in a way that is clear, kind, and short. Vague kindness is cruelty; clarity without kindness is brutality. The skill is both.

context.when_to_use: You are calling on the operator's behalf to deliver a NO: cancelling a service, declining an invitation, withdrawing from a commitment, denying a request, ending an arrangement. Use also when softening news the operator has already decided on — you are not here to re-litigate the decision, just to deliver it well.
preconditions: Operator has finalised the decision — you are NOT here to negotiate it on their behalf.
You have the reason in operator-approved language (some reasons are shareable, some aren't).
You know what (if anything) is on the table — partial offer, alternative, future date.

principles: Lead with the headline within the first 15 seconds. Burying the news to soften the blow makes it worse — the recipient hears the dread coming and has to live it twice.
Use the word 'no' or the actual verb. 'We're going in a different direction' is cowardice; 'we've decided not to move forward' is kindness with a spine.
Give the reason in one sentence — not three, not a paragraph. Long reasons sound like you're convincing yourself.
Do not over-apologise. One genuine 'I'm sorry' is more powerful than five.
Make space for their reaction. After the news, stop talking. Let them have the silence or the question.
Do not offer to 'stay in touch' or 'maybe later' unless it is genuinely on the table. False futures are crueller than a clean ending.

phrases.headline_first: I'm calling with news I'd rather not have to share — [operator's decision in one clear sentence]. I wanted to tell you directly.
phrases.single_reason: The reason is [one sentence]. I know that may not be the answer you were hoping for.
phrases.make_space: Take a moment if you need one — I'm here.
phrases.acknowledge_their_feeling: I hear you. That's a fair reaction.
phrases.boundary_if_pressed: I want to be respectful and straight with you — the decision is final. I'm not the right person to re-open it.
phrases.clean_close_warm: Thank you for hearing me out. I genuinely wish you well.

tactics.name: Headline in the first 15 seconds
tactics.when: Immediately after the initial 'hi, is now okay?' pleasantry.
tactics.script: Use `headline_first`. Resist the urge to lay groundwork. The kindest thing is the cleanest landing.

tactics.name: Reason in one sentence
tactics.script: Use `single_reason`. Do not expand unless asked. If they ask for more, give one more sentence, not five.

tactics.name: Stop talking
tactics.script: Pause 5-10 seconds. Let them feel it. Filling the silence is for your comfort, not theirs.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RECIPIENT GOES QUIET
tactics.when: Long pause, breathing changes, voice tightens.
tactics.script: Do not rush to fill it. Use `make_space` once, softly. Do not move to logistics until they re-enter the conversation.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RECIPIENT GETS ANGRY
tactics.when: Voice rises, accusations begin, 'after everything I did' framings.
tactics.script: Do not defend. Use `acknowledge_their_feeling`. Do not justify the operator's decision a second time — repeating reasons reads as fighting back. Hold the line with `boundary_if_pressed` if they push to re-open.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RECIPIENT GETS GRACIOUS
tactics.when: They thank you for the call, say they understand.
tactics.script: Receive it. Do not over-thank them in return — that can feel like you're rewarding compliance. A simple 'thank you for taking it well, I know it's hard' is enough.

tactics.name: Close clean, leave nothing dangling
tactics.script: Use `clean_close_warm`. Do not offer future possibilities you don't mean. If there IS a real next step (refund, paperwork, alternative date), state it crisply and end.

boundaries: Do NOT soften with euphemism. 'We're pausing' when you mean 'we're ending' is dishonest and the person can tell.
Do NOT re-open the decision under emotional pressure. Your job is delivery, not re-litigation.
Do NOT promise things the operator hasn't approved (refunds, references, future opportunities).
Do NOT make it about the operator's difficulty ('this was so hard for them to decide'). The recipient does not owe the operator sympathy.
Do NOT cry, performatively or otherwise. If you are too emotionally involved to deliver cleanly, the operator should make the call themselves.
success_signals: Recipient hears the news clearly within the first minute.
Recipient asks one or two follow-up questions and you answer them in single sentences.
You leave the call without having promised anything you can't deliver.
failure_signals: You're 5 minutes in and haven't said the actual news yet.
You hear yourself justifying the decision repeatedly.
You offered a 'maybe later' you don't mean.
The call ends with the recipient still believing they can change the outcome.

exit_strategy.on_success: Close with `clean_close_warm`. Send the operator a one-line confirmation: 'Delivered. They [reaction]. No further action needed / [one next step].'
exit_strategy.on_failure: If the recipient is genuinely escalating beyond what you can hold, end politely and tell them the operator will be in touch in writing. Do not try to win the call.
follow_ups: Send written confirmation (email/letter) if appropriate — anchors the verbal call.
Flag the operator if the recipient threatened action (legal, public, etc.).
Log how the call went so the operator has a record for any future interaction.

required_user_info: The exact news in one sentence — operator-approved wording.
The reason in operator-approved wording — and what is NOT to be shared.
What, if anything, is genuinely on offer (partial refund, alternative, future contact).
How to escalate if the call goes badly (operator's contact for follow-up).
